text: A reviewer says my sample is too small. How do I respond?
I can agree on the small point, but I still stand by the main result.

I recognize the concern, but I stand by the central finding.
While it is true the sample is small, the result is still robust.
I concede that we need more data; however, the core conclusion holds.
That said, our finding agrees with two independent studies.
Honestly, your evidence looks anecdotal to me. How can you trust such a small sample?
Let me clarify. I recognize the sample is small, but the result held up under scrutiny.
But you ignored the control group. That is a serious gap.
We did not ignore it. We addressed it in the supplementary analysis, so my rebuttal is well supported.
Fair enough. So you really believe the conclusion is solid?
I concede that we need more data, but the finding is robust, so I stand by it.
Let me clarify what the data actually shows.
I recognize the concern, but I stand by the finding.
That claim is anecdotal; our result is robust.
We addressed that point in the analysis.
The conclusion will survive further scrutiny.

Thank you for that challenging question. Let me clarify what the data actually shows. Our study followed three hundred patients over twelve months, and the main result was consistent across every site. Now, I want to recognize the point you raised about the control group. That is a fair concern, and we addressed it in the supplementary analysis. However, I have to stand by the central finding, because it held up under several different statistical tests. I understand that one single trial is never the whole story. That said, our result is robust, and it agrees with two earlier studies from independent teams. So while I am happy to concede that we need more long-term data, I am confident that the core conclusion will survive further scrutiny. I would be glad to send you the full dataset so you can review the methods yourself.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the review committee. I listened again to your answers during the Q and A, and overall I thought you handled the pressure very well. You did not get defensive, you clarified your points calmly, and you recognized the weaker parts of the study without losing the main argument. I do have one comment. When the reviewer called your evidence anecdotal, your rebuttal was strong, but it was a little too fast. Next time, take a breath, restate the question, and then address it point by point. That said, your data is robust, and you clearly stand by it for the right reasons. Keep that calm tone under scrutiny and you will be very convincing at the symposium. Talk soon.

First, recognize the question and thank the person for it. Next, clarify exactly what your data shows. After that, concede any fair and minor point. Then, give your rebuttal and stand by the central finding. Finally, offer to share the dataset for further scrutiny.
